That’s a massive headwind that will linger into 2026. Pricing actions and supply chain adjustments can offset part of it, but not all.

The only silver lining is that China's revenues grew +25%, and the Rest of World was up +19%. But even here, management cautioned that Tier 1 Chinese cities are softening, hinting at early signs of macro fatigue.

Future Outlook

Lululemon is essentially telling investors to wait until 2026 for a real turnaround. That’s when new product initiatives could re-ignite growth.

Some areas of focus for our new design team include maintaining momentum in performance activities, designing into several new products across lounge and social, and giving a fresh perspective to some of our most iconic items.

Source: Earnings Call

The company needs to spice things up, but it's going to take some time, and the outlook for revenue and earnings is bad, especially once we take into account the effect of tariffs.

Analysts believe EPS will return to growth in 2027, but this could prove to be too optimistic. US revenue has officially begun to fall, and even management tempered down expectations on the China revenues.

Where will growth come from?

Valuation

With the stock down over 60% since its all-time high, you may think this is a good time to buy, but I would caution against it.

Even though the valuation is more enticing now, LULU still trades pretty close to the Sector Median.

Most importantly, if we look at the forward PEG, it’s actually not that cheap, with a ratio above 1.6.

We have to price in the fact that growth is slowing and that margins could get squeezed further.

Final Thoughts

I could give Lululemon a pass if I saw conviction in management, but based on the earnings call, there’s just not much to be excited about. Fashion goes in cycles, and Lulu’s popularity may have peaked.

Competition in this sector is fierce, and now the whole logistics side is at risk because of tariffs.

It may not go down much further, but I don’t see a compelling recovery right now.

data.title: Netstreit: A Retail REIT That Keeps Growing, Standing Up To Macro Headwinds
data.description:
The Stock: A Texas-Based Retail REIT With Nationwide Penetration

Although I write about a diverse array of industries, this season I'll be increasing my focus on REITs, not just because they are the theme of a book I am writing but because I think that often they get underreported in large financial media.

With that said, not every REIT is equal, and today's niche I'm talking about is the retail REIT, a good example of one being Netstreit Corp (NYSE:NTST), who is up nearly +9% since I suggested holding on to it in my May article.

From its business profile, we can learn it is a Dallas-based REIT that "specializes in acquiring single-tenant net lease retail properties nationwide."

An example of the types of properties it owns can be found in its June 2025 investor presentation:

What I liked about this stock in my spring rating was its portfolio growth and geographic diversification, while I was hesitant about its profit margins. In fact, the firm's CEO "highlighted the company’s strategy of diversifying its portfolio, a move that received a positive response from investors," in an interview last December for the Natl Assoc. of REITs.

This followup article will explore some new data since the firm's latest earnings results, and discuss where I think the niche of retail REITs may be going.

The Thesis: The Time To Bet on Quality Retail REITs Is Now, For Future Growth

My thesis today argues that my prior hold rating should be upgraded to a buy, agreeing with today's across-the-board bullish rating consensus on Seeking Alpha, and here is why:

Despite macro headwinds in the retail space for various reasons, this REIT is well diversified across different retail categories and different US geographies, as well as showing continued portfolio growth, strong margins and FFO growth, and an attractive balance sheet risk, in addition to my expecting an additional +8% upside to the most recent closing share price.

The following rating worksheet helps visualize in a structured way how I came to this rating.

The Sector: Retail May Face Some Pain Points in 2025, Yet Signs of Resiliency Are Still There

We cannot consider this REIT without understanding the niche it is in. According to that Natl Assoc. of REITs, retail REITs can be described as:

Retail REITs own and manage retail real estate and rent space in those properties to tenants. Retail REITs include REITs that focus on large regional malls, outlet centers, grocery-anchored shopping centers and power centers that feature big box retailers.

So, unlike healthcare REITs whose tenants primarily are medical practices and or senior-care facility operators, or residential REITs who own apartment complexes, what comes to mind with this niche and its underlying tenants is whether there is population growth and consumer confidence to spend money in those stores, in regions where Netstreit owns property.

A key piece of info in this niche is what financial firm UBS said in its note this June after the ICSC 2025 Conference:

We think the key theme from ICSC 2025 is that the Retail Real Estate sector is looking to power through recent uncertainty related to tariffs and consumer health. Activity levels at the conference were elevated, driven by increased volumes of leasing and transaction conversations. Industry participants remain enthusiastic given the group has bounced back from COVID and navigated several significant bankruptcies while improving portfolio quality.

I believe such a takeaway, and I know it is a lot to digest, points to added confidence in the face of this year's macro headwinds, and that should be considered an upside factor.

However, from a future view, there is also some area of concern in the retail sector.

For instance, Reuters reported on Sept 3rd that "holiday spending by U.S. consumers is set for its steepest drop since the pandemic as shoppers — particularly Gen Z — pull back amid economic uncertainty, a PricewaterhouseCoopers survey showed."

Visually, this is what the survey results look like:

So, if this expected pullback (which is a forecast at this point based on a sample survey group) comes true, or perhaps ends up being worse than projected, retailers who depend heavily on holiday sales could suffer, and I think investors may get concerned about retail REITs.

Earlier I spoke about this firm's portfolio diversification, so what I think could mitigate the macro risks of the retail sector for this REIT is that it has several tenants that are large grocery/pharmacy chains, including CVS (CVS) and Walmart (WMT), and the fact that consumers do not just buy groceries or refill their prescriptions only during holidays means that such tenants may be less prone to a holiday pullback then a clothing or electronics store might.

Adding to that is that expected fwd revenue growth is nearly +17% for Netstreit, as opposed to just +3.5% for its sector average, so I think that also sets this REIT apart in the face of potential headwinds in the retail niche.

So, I can't help but be at least neutral here, if not slightly bullish, when it comes to the sector/industry impact on this stock.

Revenue Growth: Net Growth Of Portfolio, And Potential Tailwind From Population Growth

If we look past the macro environment, let's consider company-specific growth factors that could impact the top-line in the future.

From its Q2 commentary (pg 4), Netstreit expects to what seems like continual portfolio expansion, "increasing net investment activity guidance to $125.0 million to $175.0 million from $75.0 million to $125.0 million."

This comes on top of +21 net new investments (57 investments - 36 dispositions) in the first half of this year, according to the same quarterly results (pg 2):

As I've said about REITs before, portfolio expansion can lead to future rental income flows from those properties (assuming they all get leased) and so I would said the above data is an upside factor for Netstreit and its future revenue growth potential. Consider that commercial leases are usually for many years, so new leases signed today can drive future rental income, and new properties can lead to future leases.

I also pointed out both the geographic and tenant diversification of this REIT, which I think lessens the future downside risk of over-exposure to one state or one tenant.

The portfolio is also very active in what some may call population growth areas of the US such as the southeast/southwest including states like Texas and Florida, according to the latest investor presentation:

More population growth in those regions in the future could lead to more spending in those retail establishments, which is good news for this retail REIT.

According to a January story in Newsweek, on population growth forecasts for this REIT's home state of Texas:

Texas is due to have the third highest growth rate of any American state this decade, with its population reaching an estimated 32,463,602 in 2030, according to the latest projection from the University of Virginia's Weldon Cooper Center for Public Service.

Of course, as a former resident for several years of the Texas capital, Austin, I saw that continual population growth in person because I was part of it. Despite the side effects of potential higher rents due to demand spikes, increased traffic and pressure on public infrastructure including water/sewage systems, I think it is a positive for a retail REIT who invests into that future growth, providing the retail infrastructure needed to meet the future demand.

After all, I'm not waiting until the holidays to go to the local Walmart or CVS in Austin to buy milk and juice, but probably later that day after work, along with hundreds of other people.

With that said, there are other retail REITs that investors may be considering, so I picked 4 to compare expected future revenue growth against, and they are a sampling of retail REITS including Getty Realty (GTY), Saul Centers (BFS), FrontView REIT (FVR), and Realty Income (O).

Incidentally, I happen to own a very small, nominal amount of shares in FrontView, to get a taste of this niche myself, rather than just writing about it.

In this peer comparison, Netstreit comes out ahead with a fwd revenue growth of +16.7%, the highest in the group:

Based on the above evidence, it seems the top-line growth picture for Netstreit is rosy, and worthy of bullishness.

Profit margins: An Impressive EBITDA Margin When Compared To Other Retail REITs

I know the prior few sections were a mouthful, but I think they set the stage nicely for leading into our next topic which is somewhat shorter: profit margins.

When it comes to a REIT, I care about the EBITDA margin first because it tells me about core business profitability before depreciation expense. Net income margin, which includes depreciation expense, also matters since it can impact reported profits and that influences headlines.

From the peer data, we can see that Netstreit achieved an EBITDA margin of nearly +79%, but two other REITs beat it there. Its net margin was negative at -3%, and the only other negative one in this group was its Dallas peer FrontView:

Compared to the overall sector, though, which may include many more REITs than these, profitability data shows that Netstreit achieved an above-average EBITDA margin and that is impressive.

If you are new to REITs as an asset class, I would not immediately get spooked by the negative profit margin, since often I have seen a REIT reporting net losses yet having positive cashflow growth. So, in the next section we will cover the topic of cashflow as it is important to a REIT.

Since property expenses are a major cost to any property owner, I want to point out the following from income statement data and it shows that on a YoY basis this REIT has achieved a much higher growth in revenue than growth in property expenses, which I think is a positive trend to consider:

Given the above evidence, I am slightly on the bullish side when it comes to earnings growth potential, however the analyst consensus is calling for an EPS decline this year so that is where this REIT loses some points in my rating. By comparison, Getty Realty, who also focuses on single-tenant retail properties, has a consensus calling for EPS growth this year.

Cashflow Trends: Core Operating Cash Expected To Keep Growing, As it Has Done

In this section, we will talk about cashflow trends, and cashflow matters also because dividends are paid from cash.

Notable to mention right off the bat is that not only did this firm grow its operating cashflow but also its forward operating cashflow is expected to grow +18.3%, far above its sector average:

We can also visualize growth in the cashflow statement, and even in quarters with a reported net loss this REIT added back considerable depreciation to achieve positive operating cashflow, and also a YoY growth in this number:

Both of these are upside factors, I think, and can give extra confidence to those investors who put more weight on cashflow trends than on earnings alone.

Because this is a REIT, the metric called FFO (funds from operations) is also critical and that also saw YoY growth, which can be seen in the income statement:

Besides FFO growth, this REIT has achieved dividend growth, however only has dividend history since 2020 so its 3-year dividend CAGR is +1.8%, just below the sector average growth.

Although there is a negative EPS growth forecast for this year, from the consensus, I think FFO is a better indicator in this case. According to the Corporate Finance Institute, for instance, "real estate companies use FFO as a more accurate operating performance benchmark."

Nevertheless, negative EPS outlooks can impact headlines, and investor sentiment, so poses a downside, but in terms of fundamental business survival and dividend payouts, FFO growth is key and this REIT has proven it can do so.

Balance Sheet: A Low D/E Ratio, And Major Bank Upgrade Recently

It certainly is worth considering some balance sheet metrics, especially since many REITs often use debt financing/leverage to help grow their real estate portfolio.

When comparing debt-to-equity (D/E), here is how Netstreit fares against peers:

The positive from the above data is that Netstreit has a D/E of just 0.72, the lowest in this peer group, and therefore a much lower debt in relation to equity.

This of course is something I consider an upside factor.

Further, on the risk front, I think the risk picture lowered a bit further after a major bank upgraded this stock in July. Seeking Alpha reported on July 28th that "Bank of America Securities upgraded Netstreit's rating to Neutral from Underperform after the triple net lease REIT turned in strong Q2 results."

In addition, from Netstreit's own words, in their recent investor presentation they boasted of "abundant liquidity" and also no significant debt maturities until 2028:

So, given the combined factors above, I am inclined to be on the bullish side when it comes to this firm's balance sheet risk profile.

Valuation: Just Enough Market Confidence To Make Sense

When it comes to valuation, this being a REIT, I think a good forward-looking valuation metric is the fwd P/FFO of 14.31, which is just slightly above the sector average of 13.63, so they are almost in line with each other.

This tells me the market is expecting future FFO (funds from operations) to perform close to the sector performance.

That confidence certainly can be reassuring to investors, but is it a justified valuation?

I think so, considering that this REIT has shown strong FFO growth and EBITDA margins, and continues to grow its portfolio. So, being valued just above the sector average seems to make sense, and for this reason I am adding points to the overall rating, as a reasonably valued stock.

Future Price Target: Modest +8% Upside Expected

Using the prior valuation metric, and the consensus FFO estimate, I came up with my own future price target for this stock.

I then averaged my target with the avg target shown in Seeking Alpha and Wall St Journal, and here is the table of results:

My avg price target being $19.06 for Dec. 2025, that expects a +8% upside, which is modest at best I think, but certainly does not call for selling the stock now.

To visualize potential capital gains, if you consider the price chart below I made, a future price target of $19.06 is well above todays 200-day SMA, yet there is so much more room to grow to get back to the 5-year high reached in 2021:

Challenge To My Rating: Future Tenant Financial Problems

A challenge in the future that can impact my bullish call could come if one of this REIT's top few tenants run into highly publicized financial difficulties, putting in to question their ability to keep paying rent.

From the investor presentation, we can focus on the top 3 tenants by annualized base rent, and they are Dollar General (DG), CVS (CVS) and The Home Depot (HD).

Dollar General being their top tenant, would be the one I would be most concerned about getting into trouble, as they contribute 7.5% to ABR.

The good news for now is that based on their recent Q2 results, Dollar General saw impressive performance, with "net sales increased 5.1% to $10.7B," and with the CEO saying he is "confident in the future of this resilient business model."

So, I think such great results for a key tenant can mitigate some of this risk.

Conclusion: Another Texas Success Story, Hidden in Plain Sight

To wrap up, today I presented evidence supporting why I am upgrading this REIT to a buy after my prior hold rating, and the key drivers of upside include portfolio growth in the face of retail headwinds, margin performance, FFO growth, and a low debt in relation to equity.

In a sentence, my call to action for investors would be to consider adding this Dallas-based retail REIT to your watch list of stocks to research further, as it has shown some fundamental qualities and potential future value to be unlocked, and I think the value add here is being able to fill the need for specialized retail space in those population growth areas of the US over the next few decades, which requires a specialized expertise and close relationships with key retailers looking for space.

data.title: Torrid Holdings Has Come Back To More Sensitive Valuations But Is Too Risky
data.description:
Torrid Holdings Inc. (NYSE:CURV) reported a very negative second quarter, with comps down 7%, worsening from Q1'25.

Margins are also falling significantly, and the company carries a lot of debt. In this context, the company provided guidance for breakeven net income for the fiscal year, based on adjusted EBITDA guidance.

I believe Torrid faces significant risks going forward, related to profitability and financing. The name does not qualify for investment, and I maintain a Hold rating, despite it being down more than 65% since I last wrote about it.

Q2'25 results

Sales trend worsening: The company posted comp sales down 7%. The result was blamed on footwear restructuring and the fact that last year, a marketing campaign (the model search) occurred in Q2 but will not impact Q3 more.

However, when pairing this with comps down 3.5% in Q1 and down almost 1% in Q4, I believe it is part of a wider trend of problems. In fact, comps were down in Q2'24, so it was not a difficult quarter.

Gross margins down: Gross margins decreased 300 bps, in line with Q1. In this case, the company has returned to clearance-level margins (Q2'24 had seen 300 bps improvement from lower clearance). Torrid is becoming more promotional in an effort to curtail sales losses.

The fact that the sales decrease was coupled with more promotions is worrying. Generally, you can have one or the other, but both together imply significant challenges to generate demand.

One factor adding to the gross margin decay is that lease expenses are recorded in CoGS. Therefore, not all of the impact comes from merchandise margin, but it is still the most important factor.

Tariffs incoming: The company said that the yearly tariff impact will be $50 million (almost 10% of CoGS), but that it has already offset $40 million via pricing and sourcing. This is strange, given that gross margins are falling. The company can either raise prices or lose margins to markdowns; both cannot happen together. If prices are raised but then marked down, then the net effect is a price discount, which, coupled with higher CoGS, implies even lower margins forward.

SG&A hard to shrink even with store closures: The company's SG&A was down $6 million YoY and leveraged about 20/30 bps YoY.

However, we need to consider two things. First, most of the company's sales are digital (70% referenced in the call), implying that selling expenses decrease more linearly with sales (for example, via shipping). Second, the company has closed 10% of its stores based on YTD (and has announced that it will close as many as 25% in FY25). Store closures should lead to lower labor expenses, but even in adjusted terms, SG&A margins are not improving.

Difficult to understand capital allocation: The company repurchased $20 million in stock in the quarter, leveraging its balance sheet by losing half its cash balances ($48 million in Q4'24 and now $21 million) while it adds debt ($8 million in its credit facility).

De minimis tailwind

As de minimis exemptions were removed, Torrid may, in fact, benefit from demand shifting from platforms like Shein or Temu into its own platforms. In the space of bigger sizes, having a wide assortment is a big advantage, and Shein's and Temu's massive assortment model attacked Torrid's competitive advantage. The company, which tends to index to lower-middle-income households (comments on the call about pricing being a problem, opening cheaper price points, etc., and of a customer in the $95/100 thousand yearly income range), could benefit from this.

Too risky for purchase

Torrid is guiding for sales of about $1.02 billion, or about 8% lower than last year. That is, the decrease will worsen from here on. Comments on margin were not provided, although most retailers are guiding for a worse H2'25 than H1'25 in terms of CoGS.

The company guided for adjusted EBITDA of $80/90 million, from which we need to remove $32 million in interest costs (annualized from $8 million in Q2'25), SBC of $6 million (annualized from ~$3 million in H1'25), and D&A of $40 million (equivalent to lease payments for that figure, so a real expense despite CAPEX at 25% of that level). If we add the above, we reach basically breakeven profitability, assuming no worsening of the macro outlook.

At $1.85 per share, the name trades at a market cap of $195 million. Indeed, this seems very cheap if one compares it with net income in the $20–50 million range in the pre-pandemic period. However, today the situation is very different. The company has been unable to reach pre-pandemic profitability since FY22, and it has also added a lot of debt, moving from zero interest in FY19 to $30/35 million today.

Further, the company's financial position is not risky at the time being ($20 million in cash and about $100 million in liquidity available), but it would not be able to sustain larger losses for a prolonged period (say, 2 years).

In my opinion, Torrid Holdings Inc. stock is simply too risky, with a worsening outlook, and without considering a worsening macro.

I believe the name is still a Hold, despite lower prices, unless one wants to take a very speculative position on a comeback.
